region_of_origin: South America (Brazil); widely cultivated in Southeast Asia, Australia, and Hawaii

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium fresh passion fruit yields approximately 36 g of edible pulp and seeds, which is a typical single-serving portion.

nutrient_source.title: Passion fruit, (granadilla), purple, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #169108
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #169108 for raw purple passion fruit used as the primary nutrient anchor. This is an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Singapore HPB Foodo values for energy (73 kcal), protein (3.0 g), fat (0.3 g), carbohydrate (5.7 g), sugar (5.7 g), sodium (19 mg), dietary fibre (13.9 g), potassium (200 mg), calcium (10 mg), iron (0.6 mg), and cholesterol (0.0 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and are retained as the primary values per the input record's HPB provenance. Saturated fat adopted from USDA SR Legacy (0.059 g/100 g); input value of 0.03 g from grabfood (a forbidden source) has been replaced. Trans fat confirmed 0.0 g per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin C adopted from USDA SR Legacy (30 mg/100 g); input value of 10 mg/100 g from grabfood has been replaced. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and all remaining micronutrients populated from USDA SR Legacy #169108.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for purple passion fruit (Passiflora edulis) was located in the University of Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate derived from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, based on the low-GI profile of high-fibre tropical fruits catalogued therein (guava GI 12–29; papaya GI ~60) and the exceptionally high dietary fibre content of passion fruit (13.9 g/100 g) which substantially attenuates postprandial glucose response. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as: GI (30) × available carbohydrate per serving (5.7 g × 36 g / 100 = 2.05 g) / 100 = 0.6. This estimate is conservative and consistent with published low-GI fruit data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008).

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (200 mg/100 g), very low sodium (19 mg/100 g), and exceptional dietary fibre (13.9 g/100 g) supporting DASH dietary goals
hypertension.guidance: Passion fruit is well suited for hypertension management; its favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (approximately 10.5:1) supports vascular tone regulation, and its high fibre content contributes to blood pressure reduction consistent with DASH dietary principles — consume whole fruit with seeds and pulp to retain full fibre benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Exceptional dietary fibre (13.9 g/100 g) slowing glucose absorption, low estimated GI (~30), and moderate sugar content (5.7 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Passion fruit is one of the most diabetes-friendly fruits available; its very high fibre-to-sugar ratio substantially attenuates postprandial glucose response — always consume whole fruit with seeds and pulp intact rather than strained juice to preserve the fibre-mediated glycaemic benefit.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.059 g/100 g), zero trans fat, high dietary fibre (13.9 g/100 g), and antioxidant vitamin C (30 mg/100 g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Passion fruit presents a favourable cardiovascular profile with negligible saturated and trans fat, high soluble fibre that supports LDL cholesterol reduction, and vitamin C contributing to endothelial antioxidant defence — regular inclusion of whole passion fruit in a balanced diet is consistent with cardiovascular disease prevention guidelines.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Vitamin C (30 mg/100 g), high dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome diversity, and potassium contributing to antioxidant and anti-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Passion fruit provides vitamin C and high dietary fibre from a whole-food source, supporting antioxidant defence and gut microbiome diversity relevant to immune regulation in rheumatoid arthritis — prefer whole fruit over sweetened preparations to maximise anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Passion fruit (Passiflora edulis) is a small tropical fruit with a tough, wrinkled purple-brown exterior and intensely aromatic yellow-orange pulp filled with edible black seeds. Native to South America, it is widely cultivated across Southeast Asia, Australia, Hawaii, and the Caribbean. The pulp has a distinctive sweet-tart flavour profile with floral, citrus-like notes and a jelly-like, seedy texture. It is typically eaten raw by scooping the pulp directly from the halved fruit, or used in juices, smoothies, desserts, sauces, and yogurt. Nutritionally, passion fruit is exceptional for a fruit: it provides 13.9 g of dietary fibre per 100 g — among the highest of any commonly consumed fruit — alongside 3.0 g protein, 73 kcal energy, 5.7 g sugar, 30 mg vitamin C, 200 mg potassium, and very low sodium (19 mg). Its saturated fat content is negligible (0.059 g/100 g) and it contains no trans fat. The high fibre-to-sugar ratio gives passion fruit a low estimated glycaemic index (~30), making it particularly suitable for individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The potassium-to-sodium ratio of approximately 10.5:1 supports DASH dietary goals. Passion fruit is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. It is known as 百香果 (bai xiang guo) in Mandarin Chinese, buah markisa in Malay, and பேஷன் பழம் in Tamil. In Singapore it is commonly consumed as a fresh fruit snack or blended into beverages; whole-fruit consumption with seeds and pulp is strongly preferred over strained juice to retain the full fibre benefit.
common_user_queries: Is passion fruit good for diabetes?
Is passion fruit high in fibre?
Can I eat passion fruit if I have high blood pressure?
What is 百香果 in English?
